advantages: Scales to massive unlabeled corpora — web text, images, and logs — without proportional human annotation cost.
Learns general-purpose representations that transfer across many downstream tasks via fine-tuning or linear probing.
Pretext labels are deterministic and noise-free relative to human labeling errors on the auxiliary task itself.
Powers modern foundation models (LLMs, CLIP) that would be impractical to train fully supervised at the same scale.
Reduces labeled-data requirements at deployment: pretrain once on unlabeled data, adapt with smaller labeled sets.
disadvantages: Pretext task design matters — a poorly chosen surrogate objective may not produce representations useful for the target task.
Pretraining is compute- and data-intensive; benefits appear only when unlabeled data is abundant and downstream labels are scarce.
Evaluation is indirect during pretrain (pretext loss) — you must validate on downstream benchmarks to know representation quality.
Risk of learning shortcuts (e.g., watermark artifacts, low-level cues) that fail under distribution shift or fine-grained tasks.
Not a replacement for labeled data on the final task — downstream fine-tuning, alignment, or prompting is usually still required.
